---
title: "Data, Evidence, and Decision Making (with R)"
author: "Ayush Patel"
subtitle: "Session 1"
institute: "National Law School of India University, Bengaluru"
date: today
date-format: "DD MMM, YYYY"
embed-resources: true
bibliography: citations.bib
format: 
  revealjs:
    fig-height: 6
    fig-width: 14
    embed-resources: true
    margin-left: 50px
    margin-right: 50px
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1400
    height: 850
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    footer: "Email: ayush.ap58@gmail.com"
---

# Why should I care about *math/numbers/data* ?

> Is this even useful?

## *Predict stuff* 


::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

[How much money will I make when I graduate?]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[What will be the price of dollar against the rupee in the next fifteen days?]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[Who will win the next general elections?]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br>  
[Is this going to be a boring course?]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

:::

::: {.column}

![[The Fortune Teller by Robert Loftin Newman](https://collectionapi.metmuseum.org/api/collection/v1/iiif/11654/36952/main-image)](https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/ad/original/ap21.159.1.jpg)

:::

:::

## *Understand stuff*

::: {.columns}

::: {.column}

[Why are some people more poor than other?]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Does the Indian judiciary have in-group bias? @aabbcdgns2025bias]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[Is population growth slowing down?]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br>  
[Can I make a confident estimate about all by looking at the few?]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

:::

::: {.column}

![[Aristotle with a Bust of Homer by Rembrandt (Rembrandt van Rijn) ](https://www.metmuseum.org/art/collection/search/437394)](https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/ep/original/DP-30758-001.jpg)

:::

:::

# Essentially, we want to be right about stuff

> Lets do a small discussion. Consider the claim that is made in the following video.

## The claim
<br>

See [this video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-0X5qKkNNuw) from 1:10 to 2:01


> The Bihar government recruited X numbers of people for a certain position (police constable). The news anchor highlights that two coaching centers in Bihar claim that a total number A and B of their students respectively cleared the exam. She points out that A+B > X. Which implies there is something off about the claims of coaching centers.

## Assessing the Claim

<br>
[What is your initial intuition about the claim? Inclined to agree/disagree/unsure?]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Whichever way you lean, try to list out why it might be the correct choice.]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[Also list out why the other two choices are not possible or less likely?]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br>  
[Do you *feel* the claim may or may not be true because a particular anchor or channel is presenting it?]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

## Implicit Assumption

<br>
[Did you consider that it is possible for an individual to be enrolled in more than one coaching center? Especially if classes are online.]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Is passing the exam necessarily mean you will get an appointment letter?]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[There even might be people that would have not taken up the job even after getting an appointment letter.]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br>  
[**Do you feel more confident about your intuition about the claim now or less?**]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

## Caring about math/numbers/data helps with

<br>
[Making sense of the world around us.]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Answering questions we care about.]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[Searching for patterns leads to more interesting questions. See Introduction in @spiegelhalter2019art.]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br>  
[Saves us from being fooled (*easily*).]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

# Effectively Using math/numbers/data involves understanding how these numbers come into existence.

## What does this mean?

<br>
*It means to deeply think about:*
<br>  
[Does this number/data represent exactly what I think it does?]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Will other see it the same way too?]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[What does it exactly capture?]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}
<br>  
[What is it unable to capture?]{.fragment fragment-index='4'}

## Agreeing on something is not so easy

<br>

> If all of you independently count the number of red objects in this class, will you all arrive at the same number?

<br>  
What if you looked for maroon, magenta, or cerulean blue? 

## Agreeing on something is not so easy

<br>  

> Try to explain what is a sandwich, i.e., define Sandwich.  

::: aside
This is where I learnt that the right way to spell Sandwich is not Sandwhich or worse Sandwitch.
:::

## Does any of these fall into your definition of Sandwich? {.nostretch}


::: {#fig-sandwich layout-ncol=3}

![Is hotdog a sandwich that is actually a taco?](https://images.pexels.com/photos/12180546/pexels-photo-12180546.jpeg){height="550"}

![What about a burger?](https://images.pexels.com/photos/4749403/pexels-photo-4749403.jpeg){height="550"}

![Does a taco count?](https://images.pexels.com/photos/18574183/pexels-photo-18574183.jpeg){height="550"}

Sandwich?

:::


## So, we need precise definitions

<br>  

> Explain the case of counting number of trees from @spiegelhalter2019art. Besides arriving at appropriate definitions, the example also has a flavour of sampling and estimation. Did you notice?

<br>  

**Ideally, we would like to define the subject of our interest in a way that it *precisely* identifies our subject of interest. Nothing more, nothing less.**

## A precise/rigorous definition

<br>  
[Precisely identifies what we want to observe/record]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Stands true across space, time, people, or groups]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}

## From identifying to Measuring

<br>  

> Once we are able to define, therefore identify, what to observe/record, we can measure it. 

## Measuring is not easy either

<br>  

> Discuss the fleeting mention to the fishing nets story in @alexander2023tswdweb

*Measurements problems can arrive in many forms:*

1. Is income or a complicated index of material possessions a good measure of well-being? (proxy)
2. A consumption or income based methods good way to gauge poverty? (proxy)
3. Is asking if a person believes in equal rights for all genders is a good way to gauge society's status of women's rights? (manner of asking, revealed/stated)

# Limitations of Data as a source of knowledge

## variability

<br>  
<br>

> The things we wish to measure varies with various factors such as geography, people, time, etc. This means we have to figure out ways to handle this variability to arrive at a reliable estimate of the measure of our interest. See @spiegelhalter2019art.

## Imperfect Measurement

1. It is not always possible to measure variable of interest directly, so we must use other closely related variables.
2. Measurements get easily influenced by the way questions are asked.
3. Somethings are better to be recorded when revealed by behaviour than by stated assertion.
4. Imprecise instruments of measurements.

> Our data is an imperfect representation of the real world. And when we use this data we should be aware of the implications of those imperfections on our analysis. See @alexander2023tswdweb. We must we aware of what can be said from given data and what cannot.

## A small detour

```{r}
#| label: fig-replicative-shipman-time
#| fig-cap: A replication of death time for Harold Shipman's victims.
#| echo: false

library(tidyverse)

read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics/refs/heads/master/00-2-shipman-times/00-2-shipman-times-x.csv") |>
  ggplot(aes(Hour, Shipman)) + 
  geom_line() +
  geom_line(aes(Hour, Comparison), linetype = 2) + 
  labs(
    title = "Comparing time of death",
    subtitle = "Solid line is for deaths of Shipman's victims. Dotted line is death count of comparison group",
    x = "Hour of the day",
    y = "Number of deaths",
    caption = "Data Source: Github dspiegel29/ArtofStatistics"
  )+
  theme_minimal()


```

:::{.notes}

A couple of things to mention:

1. The data can't tell us why people died at a given time. But that something is unusual about time of death for Shipman's victims
2. Here too there is a implicit assumption, but it is more than just reasonable one. It is ok to compare the death time of patients of other local doctors. Using that group as a benchmark to see if something unusual is going on with Shipman's patients. Unless you are worried that all those doctors in that group are serial killers too.

:::

# Fighter jets and Survivourship bias

## Abraham Wald's decision

The that data was available for wald to decide where to armour the jets. Below is a replication of table from @ellenberg2014hownotwrong.

|Section of plane| Bullet holes per square foot|
|----------------|-----------------------------|
|Engine|1.11|
|Fuselage|1.73|
|Fuel system|1.55|
|Rest of the plane|1.8|

**He decided that sections with less bullet holes should have more armour than sections with more bullet holes.**

## The numbers we don't see {.smaller}

*In certain situations we only observe those cases/observations that pass through some filtration process. **Making a decision without considering those cases/observations that don't pass this filtration process can induce Survivourship bias and lead to bad decisions.***

|Section of plane| Bh/Sqf for the jests that came back| Bh/Sqf for the jests that were shot down*|
|----------------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
|Engine|1.11|1.95|
|Fuselage|1.73|1.35|
|Fuel system|1.55|1.75|
|Rest of the plane|1.8|1.2|

::: aside

*I did not go and look for shot down planes and count bullet holes. Third column is entirely made up. This is emphasize how ignoring the data that we don't see can lead us to making bad decision.
:::

## Survivourship Bias

<br>

[A decision is made by observing a cases that pass some filtering process or condition]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[This can lead to incorrect conclusion as it forces us to ignore other similar cases that do not pass the filtering or condition]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[some examples on upcoming slides]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}

## Education & careers — India{.smaller}

> *"IIT and IIM alumni are running the world's biggest companies —
> Sundar Pichai, Satya Nadella, Indra Nooyi.
> Indian engineering education clearly produces extraordinary leaders."*

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we see**

A handful of spectacularly successful alumni profiled in every
media outlet, coaching brochure, and motivational speech.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we don't see**

Over 1 million students appear for JEE each year; roughly 17,000
get into IITs. All IIT grads don't become tech CEOs or even work in engineering fields. Equally talented students who didn't get in
are never tracked at all.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Ask yourself:** Does the success of a few alumni tell you anything
about what the institution does for the *average* graduate?
Does IIT *cause* success?
:::

::: aside
Source: JEE Advanced 2024 data, NTA; profiles reported in *Economic Times*, *Forbes India*
:::

## Startups & entrepreneurship — India{.smaller}

> *"India is the world's third-largest startup ecosystem, with 1.97 lakh
> DPIIT-registered startups and dozens of unicorns like Zepto, Razorpay,
> and CRED. Entrepreneurship is a viable career path for young Indians."*

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we see**

Unicorn founders on magazine covers, "India's startup boom" headlines,
and relentless coverage of funding rounds.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we don't see**

In 2025 alone, over 11,000 Indian startups shut down — roughly
30 every single day. These closures receive almost no media coverage.
The DPIIT count includes *registered* entities, not *operating* ones.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Ask yourself:** "Registered" vs "active" vs "profitable" — which number
matters? If a journalist only interviews founders whose companies are still
running, what kind of advice will readers get?
:::

::: aside
Source: Sachin Vishkarma, "Why 90% of Indian Startups Fail," *Medium* (Dec 2025); DPIIT Startup India data
:::

## Government schemes & policy — India{.smaller}

> *"The Smart Cities Mission has been a success: Indore, Surat, and
> Bhubaneswar have been transformed. 76% of proposed projects worth
> ₹1,10,635 crore have been completed."*

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we see**

Award-winning "model" cities showcased at national events, used as
proof of concept and cited in policy reports.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we don't see**

Research finds the Mission primarily transformed small precincts within
chosen cities, not whole cities. Non-winning cities received similar
resources with far less visible results. The 845 nominations came from
only 80 self-selecting cities.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Ask yourself:** When a government evaluates a scheme using its own
award-winners, what selection bias is built in?
What would a *fair* evaluation need to include?
:::

::: aside
Source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, ISAC 2022 (*Deccan Herald*);
Bana & Bhatt, "Area-Based Urban Renewal for Smart Cities," *Urban Planning* (2021)
:::

## Clinical trials & publication bias{.smaller}

> *"Studies show this drug reduces hospitalisation by 40%
> in patients with severe illness."*

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we see**

Trials that found a significant positive effect — published in journals,
cited by regulators, reported in the news.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What we don't see**

The "file drawer problem": null results and failed trials are rarely
published. The published literature is a biased sample of all research
ever conducted. This is why drugs that look effective in early trials
often fail in larger ones.
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**The deepest version:** Survivorship bias here operates at the level
of what *knowledge gets produced* — not just what gets reported.
The missing planes never took off.
:::

::: aside
Source: Ioannidis, "Why Most Published Research Findings Are False," *PLOS Medicine* (2005);
CDSCO clinical trial regulations
:::

# Extrapolation and Obese Americans

<br>

>Discussion on Chapter 3: Everyone is Obese in @ellenberg2014hownotwrong.

## Linear Regression - concept

<br>  

[if we see a chart where a bunch of points make a pattern that looks like a line, we assume the relation between the two variable is *Linear*]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Example: travel time and distance in Bengaluru. Say, this realation looks like:]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[$Time\space in \space minutes  = 5 + (20* Distance \space in \space Kilometers)$]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}

## Linear Regression - concept{.nostretch}

```{r}
#| label: fig-linear-blr-travel
#| fig-cap: A representation of travel time and distance covered in Bengaluru
#| echo: false
#| fig-dpi: 500

tibble(
  distance = rep(c(1:25),3),
  travel_time = 5 + 20*distance + rnorm(75,7,32)
) |>
  ggplot(aes(distance, travel_time)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = F) +
  labs(
    title = "A linear relation between travel time and distance to travel",
    x = "Distance in km",
    y = "Time in minutes"
  ) +
    theme_minimal()
```

## Meet the Penguins

Details and data from @palmerpenguins

![Plamerpenguins](https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/reference/figures/lter_penguins.png)

## Fluffy Penguins{.nostretch}

$$Body \space Mass(g) = -5780.83 + 49.69*Flipper \space Length(mm)$$

```{r}
#| label: fig-penguins
#| fig-cap: We can see that penguins with larger flippers have greater body mass
#| fig-dpi: 500
#| echo: false

library(palmerpenguins)

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, 
                 shape = species),
             size = 2) +
  annotate(
    geom = "rect", xmin = 189.5, xmax=190.5, ymin = 3000, ymax = 4800, alpha = 0.2
  )+
  scale_color_manual(values = c("darkorange","darkorchid","cyan4")) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Careful while extrapolating

<br>
For the data that we have, following is true:
<br>
$$Body \space Mass(g) = -5780.83 + 49.69*Flipper \space Length(mm)$$ 

<br>  

*Does this mean a penguin with flipper length of 100mm will weigh **- 811.83 grams**??*

## Things to remember about extrapolation

<br>  
[Are you estimating beyond the range of your data?]{.fragment fragment-index='1'}
<br>  
[Is the line really a line or are you just zoomed in?]{.fragment fragment-index='2'}
<br>  
[Is the data only for a specific group? Does the realtion in the previous chart hold for baby penguins?]{.fragment fragment-index='3'}

## Class Activity 

For the examples that follow, think about: 

1. Is there data behind this claim? What would you need to verify it? 
2. What story is being told, and what might be left out?
3. Do you have clear definitions?
4. What can you answer with this information? What cannot be answered?

## Exe 1 — Courts & Judicial Administration{.smaller}

> "As on November 21, 2023, there is a pendency of 4.4 crore cases in
> the district and taluka courts, 61.7 lakh in High Courts and 79,593
> cases in the Supreme Court — a total of over five crore cases handled
> by 20,580 judges."

::: aside
**Source:** Centre for Research and Planning, Supreme Court of India,
*State of the Judiciary* (November 2023). Cited in: Satya Prakash,
"21k judges struggle to clear 5 crore cases," *Tribune India*,
December 17, 2023. Primary data: njdg.ecourts.gov.in

:::

::: {.fragment}

- What is a "pending case"? Does a case filed yesterday count the same
  as one filed thirty years ago?
- The figure is a cross-section. What would the trend over five or ten years
  tell us that this number cannot?
- Does 20,580 judges sound like enough, too few, or too many? What would
  you need to know to answer that? (The Law Commission once recommended
  50 judges per million people — India currently has around 14.)

:::

## Exe 2 — Crime Statistics {.smaller}

> "India recorded 58.86 lakh cognisable crimes in 2024, a 6% decline
> compared to 62.41 lakh cases in 2023. The crime rate per lakh
> population fell from 448.3 to 418.9."

::: aside
**Source:** National Crime Records Bureau (NCRB), Ministry of Home
Affairs, Government of India. *Crime in India 2024* (released 2025).
Primary report: ncrb.gov.in
:::

::: {.fragment}

- Is a fall in recorded crimes the same as a fall in actual crimes?
  What might cause the two to diverge?
- The same report notes that cybercrime rose 17.9% in the same year.
  How does that sit alongside the headline decline?
- Who collects this data, and who reports crimes to them? What kinds
  of crimes are likely to be under-reported, and by whom?

:::

## Exe 3 — Environment & Public Health {.smaller}

> "Air pollution is responsible for 7.2% of all deaths in India, with
> approximately 34,000 deaths annually across ten cities." — to which
> the Ministry of Environment responded in the Rajya Sabha that "there
> is no conclusive data to directly correlate air pollution and deaths."

::: aside
**Sources:** Claim: Jairam Ramesh (Congress), citing Lanvit al., "Air
Pollution and Mortality in Ten Indian Cities," *The Lancet*, June 2024.
Government response: Ministry of Environment, Forests and Climate
Change, reply to Rajya Sabha question, July 29, 2024.
:::

::: {.fragment}

- Two official sources, a peer-reviewed Lancet study and a government
  ministry, appear to contradict each other. How do you decide which
  to trust?
- What does "conclusive" mean in the context of causation? Is it
  possible to ever prove that pollution caused a specific death?
- The Lancet figure covers only ten cities. what story does this tell,
  and what does it leave out about rural or smaller urban India?

:::


## Exe 4 — Labour & Employment {.smaller}

> "An official government survey shows India's unemployment rate rose
> to a 45-year high of 6.1% in 2017–18. Joblessness stood at 7.8% in
> urban areas and 5.3% in rural areas; the unemployment rate for rural
> women was 13.6%."

::: aside
**Source:** National Sample Survey Office (NSSO), Ministry of
Statistics and Programme Implementation, Government of India.
*Periodic Labour Force Survey (PLFS) 2017–18*. Reported by: Manoj
Kumar and Devjyot Ghoshal, Reuters / *Business Standard*,
January 31, 2019. Primary data: mospi.gov.in
:::

::: {.fragment}

- What counts as "employed"? If someone works one hour a week as a
  paid domestic worker, are they unemployed?
- The headline figure is 6.1%. Rural women show 13.6%. Which number
  would you lead with, and why does the choice matter?
- This survey was reportedly withheld before its scheduled release.
  Does the details about data: who commissioned it, who released it,
  and when, affect how you read it?

:::

## Exe 5 — Economic Policy {.smaller}

> "India's GDP growth of 7.8% in Q1 FY2023–24 is not just an indicator
> of the country's growth, but explains all about India's swift march
> from a 'Fragile 5 to Top 5' economy."

::: aside
**Source:** Anurag Thakur, Minister of Information and Broadcasting,
Government of India. Reported by All India Radio / News on AIR,
"India's GDP growth rises to 7.8 percent in April to June quarter,"
August 31, 2023. Primary data: National Statistical Office, mospi.gov.in
:::

::: {.fragment}

- GDP measures the size of the economy. Growth in GDP indicated how fast the economy is growing compared to previous time period. Does it say anything
  about whether people's lives are getting better? What else would
  you want to know?
- "Fragile 5 to Top 5" is a comparison, but compared to what, and
  on which measure? How would you verify this claim?
- What can a single quarterly figure of 7.8% tell you, and what
  can it not? How might the same economy look different if you chose
  a different quarter or a different measure?

:::

# End of Session 1

# References
